title: AI Safety Formalization Atlas
description:
AI Safety Formalization Atlas is an open workbench for doing AI safety the formal way: a growing library of machine-checked Lean 4 proofs about the limits and guarantees of AI systems, with a transparent registry that records where each result is formalized, which maintained declarations it reuses, and where genuine gaps remain.

Most AI-safety reasoning lives in prose and intuition, and the rigor that does exist is scattered across incompatible proof systems. The Atlas pulls it onto one surface, so a safety argument stops being an argument and becomes a theorem you can build on, break, or extend. It is built to grow across the field — alignment, oversight, interpretability, robustness, and the paradoxes that make these problems wicked — with formalized impossibility results as the initial seed and no ceiling on scope; impossibility and possibility run on the same machinery.

Scope. Machine-checked Lean 4 formalizations span computability, logic and self-reference, social choice, and learning theory, covering both impossibility results and possibility guarantees (including a reviewed reproduction of a scalable-oversight result), exposed through a single stable root import, alongside independently reproduced external formalizations with pinned, rebuildable provenance. The current, authoritative inventory of formalized results — their status, provenance, and open gaps — is maintained in the repository's registry; this record does not enumerate it, so the description stays accurate as the library grows.

Epistemic scope. A machine-checked proof establishes its encoded mathematical statement; it does not by itself establish that the statement fully captures an informal AI-safety claim. Classical results and AI-system bridge claims are recorded as separate layers, and uncertain semantic relationships are flagged for human review. The registry reports verified coverage transparently rather than claiming complete formal coverage.

Licensed under Apache-2.0; individual external formalizations remain under their own licenses, recorded in the registry.

creators.name: Brčić, Mario
